elements.sent-2411-001: The quest for topological superconductors triggers revived interests in resolving non-$s$-wave pairing channels mediated by phonons.
elements.sent-2411-002: While density functional theory and denstify functional perturbtaion theory have established a powerful framework to calculate electron-phonon couplings in real materials in a first-principles way, its application is largely limited to conventional $s$-wave superconductivity.
elements.sent-2411-003: Here, we formulate an efficient and simple-to-use algorithm for first-principles pairing channel analysis, and apply it to several representative material systems.
elements.sent-2411-004: In the past two decades, the first-principles calculation within the framework of density functional theory (DFT) has reached a status to reliably describe not only the normal states of a wide range of materials, but also conventional superconductivity (SC) mediated by phonons~\cite{RevModPhys.89.015003 .}
elements.sent-2411-005: Calculating the electron-phonon couplings (EPCs) from first principle is rapidly reaching maturity, thanks to the development of density functional perturbation theory (DFPT)~\cite{RevModPhys.73.515 .}
elements.sent-2411-006: It is commonly known that the predominating pairing channel from phonons is of a $s$-wave symmetry, and the Cooper pairs form spin singlets.
elements.sent-2411-007: In practice, the spin degrees of freedom of EPCs are thus ignored in most first-principles calculations.
elements.sent-2411-008: To predict the superconducting transition temperature (T$_c$), the $s$-wave channel is implicitly assumed, either in McMillan-Allen-Dynes formula~\cite{mcmillan1968transition or the self-consistent solution of Migdal-Eliashberg equations~\cite{eliashberg1960interactions .}}
elements.sent-2411-009: Recently, the potential role of phonons in achieving unconventional superconductivity attracts revived theoretical attentions.
elements.sent-2411-010: The concern is that while electron-electron Coulomb repulsion always suppresses the conventional $s$-wave pairing, it can be collaborative with phonons in stabilizing other pairing symmetries, e.g. in a topological superconductor~\cite{fu2010odd,Wan,brydon2014odd , or in high-Tc cuprate and pnictides superconductors~\cite{PhysRevB.104.L140506,PhysRevB.83.092505,shen2002role .}}
elements.sent-2411-011: In these cases, the subleading non-$s$-wave pairing channels from EPC should be taken into account.
elements.sent-2411-012: First-principles analysis along this direction is very limited.
elements.sent-2411-013: The pioneering calculation by Wan and Savrasov~\cite{Wan on doped Bi$_2$Se$_3$ employed a set of orthogonal polynomials defined on the Fermi surface (FS), known as FS harmonics~\cite{PhysRevB.13.1416 , to project EPC to different pairing channels.}}
elements.sent-2411-014: The results clearly indicate that non-$s$-wave pairing information is readily encoded in the standard DFPT EPC data.
elements.sent-2411-015: However, the construction of FS harmonics is tedious, especially for complicated FS's.
elements.sent-2411-016: The functions depend on not only the crystal structure, but also FS geometry, which hinders high-throughput applications of the method.
elements.sent-2411-017: Nomoto et al.~\cite{Tokura extended DFT for superconductivity (SCDFT)~\cite{PhysRevB.72.024545,PhysRevB.72.024546 to deal with odd-parity pairing in an ab initio way.}}
elements.sent-2411-018: The treatment is exact when $S_z$ is a good quantum number, e.g. without the spin-orbit coupling (SOC).
elements.sent-2411-019: When SOC is strong, however, the exact forms of odd-parity pairing in the basis of Kohn-Sham (K-S) eigenstates are not known a priori.
elements.sent-2411-020: The primary goal of this Article is to formulate an efficient and simple-to-use algorithm for first-principles EPC pairing-channel analysis.
elements.sent-2411-021: The key is to properly solve gauge indeterminancy of the EPCs.
elements.sent-2411-022: After that, we show that the pairing channels can be resolved by standard matrix diagonalization.
elements.sent-2411-023: We call this approach the ``direct diagonalization method'' (DDM).. In Sec.
elements.sent-2411-024: II, we will first review the general theory of phonon-mediated pairing interactions, and then discuss the gauge complexity.
elements.sent-2411-025: In the end, we present the DDM method.. In Sec.
elements.sent-2411-026: III, the DDM method is applied to several realistic material systems.
elements.sent-2411-028: We start by considering a system with inversion ($\mathcal{I}$) and time-reversal ($\mathcal{T}$) symmetries.
elements.sent-2411-029: Without $\mathcal{I}$, the definition of parity is missing, and the gap function is in general a mixture of odd and even parities.
elements.sent-2411-030: Without $\mathcal{T}$, the scenario is complicated by the coexistence of magnetism, which we defer for future works.
elements.sent-2411-031: The combination of $\mathcal{I}$ and $\mathcal{T}$ enforces that every eigenstate is at least doubly degenerate.
elements.sent-2411-032: Every K-S eigenstates can then be labeled by a band index $n$, the lattice momentum $\mathbf{k}$ and a pseudospin variable $s=\pm$.
elements.sent-2411-033: The DFT+DFPT calculations quantify the following EPC Hamiltonian: \begin{eqnarray}H_{ep}=\sum_{n,m,\mathbf{k,q},s,s'\upsilon}g_{n\mathbf{k}s,m\mathbf{k+q}s'}^{\upsilon}c_{m\mathbf{k+q}s'}^{\dagger}c_{n\mathbf{k}s}(b_{\upsilon,\mathbf{q}}^{\dagger}+b_{\upsilon ,-\mathbf{q}}), \nonumber\end{eqnarray} in which $c_{n\mathbf{k s}$ ($b_{\upsilon,q}$) is the annihilation operator of the electron (phonon) eigenstate.}
elements.sent-2411-034: The phonons are labeled by a mode index $\upsilon$ and the lattice vection $\mathbf{q}$.
elements.sent-2411-035: Within the second-order perturbation, the EPC generates the pairing interaction: \begin{eqnarray}\label{eq:Hpair} H_{pair}=-\sum_{n\mathbf{k},m\mathbf{k}',s_{1,2,3,4}}V_{s_2,s_1;s_3,s_4}(n\mathbf{k},m\mathbf{k}') \nonumber c_{m\mathbf{k}'s_3}^{\dagger}c_{m,\mathbf{-k}',s_4}^{\dagger}c_{n,\mathbf{-k},s_2}c_{n\mathbf{k}s_1},\end{eqnarray} with \begin{eqnarray}\label{eq:V} V_{s_2,s_1;s_3,s_4}(n\mathbf{k},m\mathbf{k}') = \sum_\upsilon \frac{g_{n\mathbf{k}s_1,m\mathbf{k'}s_3}^{\upsilon}g_{n\mathbf{-k}s_2,m\mathbf{-k'}s_4}^{\upsilon}}{\omega_{\mathbf{k'}-\mathbf{k},\upsilon}} \nonumber \times \delta(\epsilon_{n\mathbf{k}}-\epsilon_F)\delta(\epsilon_{m\mathbf{k}’}-\epsilon_F),\end{eqnarray} in which $\epsilon_{n\mathbf{k }$ ($\omega_{\mathbf{k' -\mathbf{k ,\upsilon}$) is the electron (phonon) eigen-energy, and we restrict the involved electrons residing at the Fermi energy ($\epsilon_F$).}}}
elements.sent-2411-036: To bridge the interaction to a matrix analysis problem, it is convenient to view $V_{s_2,s_1;s_3,s_4 (n\mathbf{k ,m\mathbf{k ')$ as the components of a matrix $\mathbb{V}$ and define a vector operator $\hat \Delta$ with its components \begin{eqnarray}\label{eq:Delta} \hat \Delta(n\mathbf{k}s_1s_2)=c_{n,\mathbf{-k},s_2}c_{n\mathbf{k}s_1},\end{eqnarray} which physically annihilates a Copper pair consisting of electrons at the $|n\mathbf{k s_1\rangle$ and $|n,\mathbf{-k ,s_2\rangle$ states.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-037: Its ground-state expectation value $\langle \hat \Delta(n\mathbf{k s_1s_2)\rangle$ reflects the SC order parameter.}
elements.sent-2411-038: Eq.~(\ref{eq:Hpair ) is then transformed into a compact quadratic form: \begin{eqnarray}H_{pair}=-\hat \Delta^\dag\mathbb{V}\hat \Delta.\end{eqnarray}.}
elements.sent-2411-039: Roughly, diagonalizing the kernel $\mathbb{V}$ is supposed to decompose different pairing channels.
elements.sent-2411-040: The complexity is that EPCs are routinely evaluated on a $k$-mesh in the full Brillouin zone, where K-S orbitals at each $k$ point is treated independently with ``gauge'' indeterminacy.
elements.sent-2411-041: It is straightforward to see that $\hat \Delta(n\mathbf{k s_1s_2)$ is gauge dependent by adding an arbitrary U(1) phase to $c_{n\mathbf{k s_1}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-042: More critically, the double degeneracy allows any recombination of the degenerate K-S orbitals as the eigenstate.
elements.sent-2411-043: Therefore, an arbitrary SU(2) rotation usually also presents in the first-principles data, especially for strong SOC systems, which hinders a textbook definition of the pairing symmetry assuming $s_1,s_2$ in $\hat \Delta$ refer to a global spin axis.
elements.sent-2411-044: It is worth mentioning that maximally localized Wannier function interpolation is a powerful procedure for ab initio gauge fixing~\cite{RevModPhys.84.1419 , but to properly specify the pseudo spin index, especially for strong SOC systems, could still be tricky.}
elements.sent-2411-045: The main advantage of the DDM method is to avoid specifying the gauge condition, such that the computational procedure could label the pairing channel in any gauge.
elements.sent-2411-046: Let us denote \begin{eqnarray}\label{eq:T} \mathcal{T}c_{n\mathbf{k}s}\equiv s\tilde{c}_{n,-\mathbf{k},-s},\end{eqnarray} in which the sign $s$ before $\tilde{c}$ is used to explicitly track the $\mathcal{T ^2=-1$ condition for an electron.}
elements.sent-2411-047: The tilde symbol implies that $(\tilde{c _{n,-\mathbf{k ,+ , \tilde{c _{n,-\mathbf{k ,- )$ is allowed to differ from $(c_{n,-\mathbf{k ,+ , c_{n,-\mathbf{k ,- )$, i.e. the numerical K-S states at $-\mathbf{k}$ without gauge fixing, by an arbitrary U(1)$\times$SU(2) transformation.}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-048: Thus defined $\tilde{c _{n,-\mathbf{k ,-s}$ also relates to $c_{n\mathbf{k ,-s}$ via spatial inversion as: \begin{eqnarray}\label{eq:I} \mathcal{I}c_{n\mathbf{k},-s}=e^{i\alpha_{n\mathbf{k}}}\tilde{c}_{n,-\mathbf{k},-s}.\end{eqnarray}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-049: Note that theoretical analysis usually implicitly assumes $e^{i\alpha_{n\mathbf{k =1$, which however does not automatically hold for the numerical K-S states.}}}
elements.sent-2411-050: A U(1) transformation $c_{n\mathbf{k}\pm}\rightarrow e^{i\alpha_{n\mathbf{k}\pm c_{n\mathbf{k}\pm}$ will lead to an overall phase shift $\alpha_{n\mathbf{k =\alpha_{n\mathbf{k + +\alpha_{n\mathbf{k -}$.. We rewrite Eqs.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-051: The emergence of summand sign $s_2s_4$ is a direct consequence of $\mathcal{T ^2=-1$ [c.f.. Eq. (\ref{eq:T )].}}
elements.sent-2411-052: A useful point is that the EPC between $\tilde c_{m,\mathbf{-k ',s' ^{\dagger}$ and $\tilde c_{n,\mathbf{-k ,s}$ can be readily obtained from that between $c_{m,\mathbf{k ',-s' ^{\dagger}$ and $ c_{n,\mathbf{k ,-s}$ by applying $\mathcal{T}$, so the tilde states never need to be explicitly constructed.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-054: This definition greatly facilitates numerical analysis of gap function symmetry, because its transformation under $\mathcal{I}$ is well-defined.
elements.sent-2411-056: Note that the undertermined U(1) phase $e^{i\alpha_{n\mathbf{k }$ cancels.}}
elements.sent-2411-057: Now, we can construct one even-parity operator: \begin{eqnarray}\hat\Delta^S(n\mathbf{k})=\frac{1}{2}[\hat\Delta(n\mathbf{k}+-)-\hat\Delta(n\mathbf{k}-+)],\end{eqnarray} and three odd-parity operators \begin{eqnarray}\hat\Delta^{T_0}(n\mathbf{k}) = \frac{1}{2}[\hat\Delta(n\mathbf{k}+-)+\hat\Delta(n\mathbf{k}-+)] \nonumber \hat\Delta^{T_{\pm}}(n\mathbf{k}) = \hat\Delta(n\mathbf{k}\pm\pm).\end{eqnarray}.
elements.sent-2411-058: Transforming into this basis, the kernel $\mathbb{V}$ is block diagonalized into the even-parity singlet sector ($\mathbb{V ^S$) and the the odd-parity triplet sector ($\mathbb{V ^T$).}}
elements.sent-2411-059: The matrix elements are: \begin{eqnarray}V^S(n\mathbf{k},m\mathbf{k}') = \sum_{\upsilon s_1s_3}\frac{|g_{n\mathbf{k}s_1,m\mathbf{k'}s_3}^{\upsilon}|^2}{\omega_{\mathbf{k'}-\mathbf{k},\upsilon}} \nonumber \times \delta(\epsilon_{n\mathbf{k}}-\epsilon_F)\Theta(\epsilon_{m\mathbf{k}’}-\epsilon_F),\end{eqnarray} and \begin{widetext \begin{eqnarray}\label{eq:VT} V^T(n\mathbf{k},m\mathbf{k}') = \sum_\upsilon \begin{pmatrix} g_{n\mathbf{k}+,m\mathbf{k'}+}^{\upsilon}(g_{n\mathbf{k}-,m\mathbf{k'}-}^{\upsilon})^* -\sum_s g_{n\mathbf{k}+,m\mathbf{k'}s}^{\upsilon}(g_{n\mathbf{k}-,m\mathbf{k'}s}^{\upsilon})^* -g_{n\mathbf{k}+,m\mathbf{k'}-}^{\upsilon}(g_{n\mathbf{k}-,m\mathbf{k'}+}^{\upsilon})^* -\sum_s g_{n\mathbf{k}s,m\mathbf{k'}+}^{\upsilon}(g_{n\mathbf{k}s,m\mathbf{k'}-}^{\upsilon})^* \sum_{ ss'}ss'|g_{n\mathbf{k}s_1,m\mathbf{k'}s_3}^{\upsilon}|^2 -\sum_s g_{n\mathbf{k}s,m\mathbf{k'}-}^{\upsilon}(g_{n\mathbf{k}s,m\mathbf{k'}+}^{\upsilon})^* -g_{n\mathbf{k}-,m\mathbf{k'}+}^{\upsilon}(g_{n\mathbf{k},+,m\mathbf{k'}-}^{\upsilon})^* -\sum_s g_{n\mathbf{k}-,m\mathbf{k'}s}^{\upsilon}(g_{n\mathbf{k}+,m\mathbf{k'}s}^{\upsilon})^* g_{n\mathbf{k}-,m\mathbf{k'}-}^{\upsilon}(g_{n\mathbf{k}+,m\mathbf{k'}+}^{\upsilon})^* \end{pmatrix} \nonumber \times \frac{\delta(\epsilon_{n\mathbf{k}}-\epsilon_F)\Theta(\epsilon_{m\mathbf{k}’}-\epsilon_F)}{\omega_{\mathbf{k'}-\mathbf{k},\upsilon}}.\end{eqnarray}.}
elements.sent-2411-060: The weak-SOC approximation employed by Nomoto et al. \cite{Tokura to evaluate odd-parity pairing can be viewed as neglecting the off-diagonal matrix elements in Eq. (\ref{eq:VT ).}}
elements.sent-2411-061: Without introducing these approximations, we proceed by directly diagonalizing $\mathbb{V ^{S(T)}$: \begin{eqnarray}\mathbb{V}^{S}|\sigma_i\rangle = \lambda^S_i|\sigma_i\rangle, \nonumber \mathbb{V}^{T}|\tau_i\rangle = \lambda^T_i|\tau_i\rangle.\end{eqnarray}.}
elements.sent-2411-062: In practice, we non-dimensionlize $\mathbb{V}$ by $V_{s_2,s_1;s_3,s_4 (n\mathbf{k ,m\mathbf{k ')\rightarrow V_{s_2,s_1;s_3,s_4 (n\mathbf{k ,m\mathbf{k ')N_{n\mathbf{k }$, where $N_{n\mathbf{k }$ is the contribution of the $|n\mathbf{k s\rangle$ state to the total density of states.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-063: This makes the largest $\lambda^S_i$, which we denote as $\lambda^S_{max}$, directly comparable to the isotropic dimensionless EPC strength $\lambda$ reported in literature.
elements.sent-2411-064: The largest $\lambda^T_i$ ($\lambda^T_{max}$) gives the leading odd-parity spin-triplet pairing strength.
elements.sent-2411-065: The eigenvectors: \begin{eqnarray}\label{eq:eigenvector} |\sigma_i\rangle = [\xi_i(n_1\mathbf{k}_1), \xi_i(n_1\mathbf{k}_2), ...]^T, |\tau_i\rangle = [\xi_i(T_0,n_1\mathbf{k}_1), \xi_i(T_+,n_1\mathbf{k}_1), \xi_i(T_-,n_1\mathbf{k}_1), ...]^T \nonumber\end{eqnarray} reflect the $\mathbf{k}$-space symmetry of the corresponding pairing channel, which can be classified by the irreducible representations (irreps.) of the symmetry group.

elements.sent-2411-077: Below we apply DDM to several representative materials.

elements.sent-2411-119: In summary, we present an efficient and simple-to-use algorithm for first-principles EPC pairing-channel analysis.
elements.sent-2411-120: It is theoretically known that the leading pairing channel by EPC is $s$-wave \cite{brydon2014odd .}
elements.sent-2411-121: All our first-principles results support this perception.
elements.sent-2411-122: Meanwhile, the calculations also indicate that EPC contains non-negligible non-$s$-wave channels.
elements.sent-2411-123: These quantifications and further applications in a high-throughput manner are expected to provide useful guide to realize exotic superconductivity.
elements.sent-2411-124: While density functional theory and density functional perturbation theory have established a powerful framework to calculate electron-phonon couplings in real materials in a first-principles way, its application is largely limited to conventional $s$-wave superconductivity.
elements.sent-2411-125: The concern is that while electron-electron Coulomb repulsion always suppresses the conventional $s$-wave pairing, it can be collaborative with phonons in stabilizing other pairing symmetries, e.g., in a topological superconductor~\cite{fu2010odd,Wan,brydon2014odd , or in high-$T_c$ cuprate and pnictides superconductors~\cite{PhysRevB.104.L140506,PhysRevB.83.092505,shen2002role .}}
elements.sent-2411-126: In these cases, the non-$s$-wave pairing channels from EPC should be taken into account.
elements.sent-2411-127: Nomoto $et$ $al.$~\cite{Tokura extended DFT for superconductivity (SCDFT)~\cite{PhysRevB.72.024545,PhysRevB.72.024546 to deal with odd-parity pairing in an ab initio way.}}
elements.sent-2411-128: The treatment is exact when $S_z$ is a good quantum number, e.g., without the spin-orbit coupling (SOC).
elements.sent-2411-129: When SOC is strong, however, the exact forms of odd-parity pairing in the basis of Kohn-Sham (KS) eigenstates are not known a priori.
elements.sent-2411-130: The primary goal of this article is to formulate an efficient and simple-to-use algorithm for first-principles EPC pairing-channel analysis.
elements.sent-2411-131: The key is to properly solve gauge indeterminacy of the EPCs.
elements.sent-2411-132: In the end, we present the DDM.. In Sec.
elements.sent-2411-133: III, the DDM is applied to several realistic material systems.
elements.sent-2411-135: Every KS eigenstate can then be labeled by a band index $n$, the lattice momentum $\mathbf{k}$ and a pseudospin variable $s=\pm$.
elements.sent-2411-136: The DFT+DFPT calculations quantify the following EPC Hamiltonian: \begin{eqnarray}H_{ep}=\sum_{n,m,\mathbf{k,q},s,s'\upsilon}g_{n,\mathbf{k},s;m,\mathbf{k+q},s'}^{\upsilon}c_{m,\mathbf{k+q},s'}^{\dagger}c_{n,\mathbf{k},s}(b_{\upsilon,-\mathbf{q}}^{\dagger}+b_{\upsilon ,\mathbf{q}}), \nonumber\end{eqnarray} in which $c_{n,\mathbf{k ,s}$ ($b_{\upsilon,\mathbf{q }$) is the annihilation operator of the electron (phonon) eigenstate.}}
elements.sent-2411-137: The phonons are labeled by a mode index $\upsilon$ and the lattice vector $\mathbf{q}$.
elements.sent-2411-138: Within the second-order perturbation, the EPC generates the pairing interaction: \begin{eqnarray}\label{eq:Hpair} H_{pair}=-\frac{1}{2}\sum_{n,\mathbf{k},m,\mathbf{k}',s_{1,2,3,4}}V_{s_1,s_2;s_3,s_4}(n,\mathbf{k};m,\mathbf{k}') \nonumber c_{m,\mathbf{k}',s_3}^{\dagger}c_{m,\mathbf{-k}',s_4}^{\dagger}c_{n,\mathbf{-k},s_2}c_{n,\mathbf{k},s_1},\end{eqnarray} with \begin{eqnarray}\label{eq:V} V_{s_1,s_2,s_3,s_4}(n,\mathbf{k};m,\mathbf{k}') = 2\sum_\upsilon \frac{g_{n,\mathbf{k},s_1;m,\mathbf{k'},s_3}^{\upsilon}g_{n,\mathbf{-k},s_2;m,\mathbf{-k'},s_4}^{\upsilon}}{\omega_{\mathbf{k'}-\mathbf{k},\upsilon}} \nonumber \times \delta(\epsilon_{n,\mathbf{k}}-\epsilon_F)\delta(\epsilon_{m,\mathbf{k}’}-\epsilon_F),\end{eqnarray} in which $\epsilon_{n,\mathbf{k }$ ($\omega_{\mathbf{k' -\mathbf{k ,\upsilon}$) is the electron (phonon) eigen-energy, and we restrict the involved electrons residing at the Fermi energy ($\epsilon_F$).}}}
elements.sent-2411-139: We do not consider strong coupling systems beyond this treatment.
elements.sent-2411-140: To bridge the interaction to a matrix analysis problem, it is convenient to view $V_{s_1,s_2;s_3,s_4 (n,\mathbf{k ;m,\mathbf{k ')$ as the components of a matrix $\mathbb{V}$ and define a vector operator $\hat \Delta$ with its components \begin{eqnarray}\label{eq:Delta} \hat \Delta(n,\mathbf{k},s_1,s_2)=c_{n,\mathbf{-k},s_2}c_{n,\mathbf{k},s_1},\end{eqnarray} which physically annihilates a Copper pair consisting of electrons at the $|n,\mathbf{k ,s_1\rangle$ and $|n,\mathbf{-k ,s_2\rangle$ states.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-141: Its ground-state expectation value $\langle \hat \Delta(n,\mathbf{k ,s_1,s_2)\rangle$ reflects the SC order parameter.}
elements.sent-2411-142: Eq.~(\ref{eq:Hpair ) is then transformed into a compact quadratic form: \begin{eqnarray}H_{pair}=-\frac{1}{2}\hat \Delta^\dagger\mathbb{V}\hat \Delta.\end{eqnarray}.}
elements.sent-2411-143: The complexity is that EPCs are routinely evaluated on a $k$-mesh in the full Brillouin zone, where KS orbitals at each $k$ point are treated independently with ''gauge'' indeterminacy.

elements.sent-2411-207: To do so, we apply an additional SU(2) transformation to rotate the spin expectation value of the Kohn-Sham wavefunctions to align a fixed axis.
elements.sent-2411-208: The same SU(2) transformation rotates the phase-fixed $\xi(T_0,n,\mathbf{k )\sigma_z$ into a non-diagonal 2x2 matrix: \begin{equation}\hat\xi’_i(n,\mathbf{k}) = \begin{pmatrix} \xi’_{i,\uparrow',\downarrow'}(n,\mathbf{k}) \xi'_{i,\uparrow',\uparrow'}(n,\mathbf{k}) \xi'_{i,\downarrow',\downarrow'}(n,\mathbf{k}) \xi'_{i,\downarrow',\uparrow'}(n,\mathbf{k}) \end{pmatrix},\end{equation} in which the pseudo spin labels $\uparrow',\downarrow'$, have been globally aligned.}
elements.sent-2411-209: The d-vector is then defined as \begin{equation}\mathbf{d}(n,\mathbf{k})= \begin{pmatrix} \frac{\xi'_{i,\uparrow',\uparrow'}(n,\mathbf{k})+\xi'_{i,\downarrow',\downarrow'}(n,\mathbf{k})}{\sqrt{2}} \frac{i\xi'_{i,\uparrow',\uparrow'}(n,\mathbf{k})-i\xi'_{i,\downarrow',\downarrow'}(n,\mathbf{k})}{\sqrt{2}} \frac{\xi'_{i,\uparrow',\downarrow'}(n,\mathbf{k})-\xi'_{i,\downarrow',\uparrow'}(n,\mathbf{k})}{\sqrt{2}} \end{pmatrix}.\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-213: It is known that by adopting Migdal-Eliashberg theory modern first-principles superconductivity calculations are able to take into account the full frequency dependence of phonon-mediated electron-electron interactions, while the present DDM is formulated at the static mean-field limit.
elements.sent-2411-214: In prospect, the DDM eigen-channels could be used as the initial guess for the self-consistent Migdal-Eliashberg calculation to target unconventional solutions, but a complete formulation requires future efforts.
